text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Hannah Keane
St Bartholomew’s Hospital
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE

Dear Dr Hannah Keane,

Liam Turner, DOB: 14-03-2003, Hospital No: H932774 NHS No. 4098573216
12 Alder Court, Mile End, London E3 4HT

Diagnoses:

Recurrent seizure episodes of uncertain classification (onset 2023)
Normal brain MRI (January 2024)

Current Medication:

Levetiracetam 500 mg bd

Plan:

Optimise sleep hygiene and reduce prolonged continuous screen exposure
Continue Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily and review in 6 months
Provide seizure safety advice and sports participation guidance

I reviewed Liam Turner in clinic today. He is a university esports club lead and reports prolonged screen time due to training, competition schedules, and event administration. He describes brief events characterised by a rising epigastric sensation, momentary déjà vu, and preserved awareness, occasionally followed by mild right-hand tingling without spread. He reports a frequency of ≤ three simple partial seizures per week, usually clustering during weeks with late-night tournaments and irregular sleep. There are no witnessed convulsions, tongue biting, or incontinence. Post-event fatigue is mild and short-lived.

Since starting Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily in May 2024, he notes a reduction in intensity and duration of episodes, though frequency remains tied to sleep disruption and extended sessions at dual monitors with high visual load. He denies mood change or irritability on current dose. No alcohol misuse or recreational drugs. He maintains academic attendance and coaching responsibilities.

Neurological examination today is normal. Prior MRI brain (January 2024) was reported as normal. A routine EEG (February 2024) was non-diagnostic. We discussed the pattern of triggers, particularly irregular sleep and prolonged continuous screen time with limited breaks. He will implement protected sleep windows, scheduled screen breaks (every 45–60 minutes), blue-light reduction settings in the evenings, and avoidance of overnight events where possible. He will track events alongside sleep duration and training intensity to help correlate triggers.

I have advised continuation of Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily. We agreed to reassess in 6 months, earlier if there is any evolution in semiology, loss of awareness, injury, or generalisation. He has been provided with seizure first-aid information for teammates and guidance on safe participation in esports tournaments and travel planning. DVLA guidance was discussed with respect to awareness-preserving events; he currently reports preserved awareness and no events at the wheel, and he does not drive.

I would be grateful if you could continue prescribing Levetiracetam 500 mg bd. Please let me know if there are any concerns regarding mood or adherence. I remain happy to review sooner should the pattern change or if he wishes to consider ambulatory EEG during tournament periods.

With best wishes,

entities.text: He reports a frequency of ≤ three simple partial seizures per week
≤ three simple partial seizures per week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'He reports a frequency of ≤ three simple partial seizures per week'. This provides a numeric upper bound on weekly frequency. No other conflicting frequencies are provided. Following the rule to prioritize explicit numeric expressions and to select the highest frequency when multiple exist, we use the upper bound as the frequency label.
seizure_frequency_number: He reports a frequency of ≤ three simple partial seizures per week
reference: ≤ three simple partial seizures per week
